names.common_name: Copper complexes of chlorophylls and chlorophyllins
names.scientific_name: Copper complexes of chlorophyll derivatives / Sodium copper chlorophyllin
names.japanese_label_name: 銅クロロフィリンナトリウム

natural_occurrence.description: Semi-synthetic derivative of natural chlorophyll. While base chlorophyll is extracted from plants (spinach, alfalfa, nettles), the copper complex does not occur in nature. Created by replacing magnesium ion with copper, which makes the green colour 10-100x more stable to heat, acid, and light than natural chlorophyll.

manufacturing.description: Chlorophyll is extracted from green plant materials (spinach, alfalfa, nettles) using food-grade solvents. The natural magnesium ion in the porphyrin ring is then replaced with copper through chemical treatment with copper salts. E141(i) is oil-soluble (retains phytol chain). E141(ii) is water-soluble (phytol chain hydrolyzed off). The copper stabilizes the green color and allows it to withstand pasteurization (85°C+) without degradation.

other_uses.cosmetics: Natural-looking green colorant in cosmetic products

eu.max_level_mg_kg: quantum satis in specific food categories
eu.conditions: ADI WITHDRAWN in 2015 due to insufficient safety data, but approval maintained at quantum satis levels

usa.notes: Approved for use in food products

japan.japanese_regulation_name: 銅クロロフィリンナトリウム
japan.notes: Approved as designated additive (chemically modified natural)

canada.notes: Approved for use in food products

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: withdrawn (2015 - insufficient data on ADME, genotoxicity, chronic toxicity, carcinogenicity)

regulatory_history.event: JECFA established ADI of 0-15 mg/kg body weight per day
EFSA withdrew ADI due to insufficient data on absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME), genotoxicity, chronic toxicity, and carcinogenicity; approval maintained but data gaps identified

food_categories_eu.category_name: Vegetables in vinegar, oil or brine

commonly_found_in: Canned peas (mushy peas) - retains green colour during canning

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA ADI of 0-15 mg/kg bw/day (2008). Note: EFSA withdrew ADI in 2015 due to insufficient long-term safety data, though approval was maintained. The copper ion is firmly bound and generally considered unlikely to be harmful. Widely used specifically in canned vegetables because copper-stabilized chlorophyll survives the high-temperature canning process (85°C+) without turning olive/brown like natural chlorophyll.
